title: Overview
text: The Neptune-9 buoy array is a distributed oceanographic sensor network monitoring temperature, salinity, and current velocity across a designated section of open ocean. Each buoy carries a sealed instrument payload, a satellite communication module, and a solar-and-wave hybrid power system designed to operate unattended for years at a time. Buoys are moored at fixed positions using a subsurface anchor and a flexible tether, allowing each buoy to track surface wave motion without drifting from its assigned station. The array's design prioritizes long-term unattended operation over real-time responsiveness, since physically servicing a buoy requires a dedicated research vessel and favorable weather. Data collected by each buoy is transmitted via satellite uplink at scheduled intervals rather than continuously, to conserve the buoy's limited stored energy budget. Neptune-9 is one of several similarly designed arrays operated by the same research consortium, each covering a different ocean region.

title: Sensor payload
text: Each buoy's instrument payload includes a conductivity-temperature-depth sensor, an acoustic Doppler current profiler, and a set of surface meteorological sensors measuring wind speed and barometric pressure. The conductivity-temperature-depth sensor derives salinity indirectly from conductivity and temperature measurements, following a standard oceanographic conversion formula rather than measuring salinity directly. The acoustic Doppler current profiler measures current velocity at multiple depths simultaneously by timing the Doppler shift of acoustic pulses reflected off particles suspended in the water column. Surface meteorological sensors sit above the waterline on a mast extending from the buoy's main hull, positioned to minimize interference from wave spray during rough conditions. All sensor readings are timestamped and logged locally before transmission, so a temporary satellite outage does not result in permanent data loss for that period.

title: Power system
text: Each buoy draws power from a combined solar panel and wave-energy harvesting system, backed by a battery bank sized to sustain several weeks of operation without any charging input at all. Solar panels provide the majority of energy input during daylight hours in typical conditions, while the wave-energy harvester contributes a smaller but steadier supplement, including during nighttime hours. The buoy's onboard power management system prioritizes sensor operation and satellite transmission over any non-essential subsystem whenever stored energy drops below a configured threshold. During extended periods of heavy cloud cover or unusually calm seas, some buoys have reduced transmission frequency automatically to conserve remaining battery capacity. Battery degradation over multiple years of operation is a known factor limiting a buoy's practical unattended lifetime before a maintenance visit becomes necessary.

title: Satellite telemetry
text: Each buoy transmits a compressed data packet via a low-earth-orbit satellite uplink at scheduled intervals, typically every four hours under normal operating conditions. Compression reduces typical transmission size substantially compared to raw sensor data, which matters directly for both transmission time and the buoy's limited energy budget per uplink session. A buoy that fails to establish a satellite connection during its scheduled window retries at the next scheduled interval rather than continuously retrying, to avoid wasting energy on a connection attempt likely to fail again immediately. Data received at the consortium's ground station is validated against expected value ranges before being published to the array's public data feed, flagging any reading outside plausible physical bounds for manual review. Historical data gaps in the public feed are almost always attributable to a scheduled transmission window that failed to connect, rather than a sensor malfunction.

title: Biofouling mitigation
text: Marine biofouling, the accumulation of algae, barnacles, and other organisms on submerged buoy surfaces, is one of the most persistent maintenance challenges for any long-term ocean deployment. Heavy biofouling on the conductivity-temperature-depth sensor's intake can bias salinity and temperature readings, which is why the array's design includes a copper-alloy anti-fouling housing around the most sensitive sensor surfaces. The acoustic Doppler current profiler is comparatively less affected by biofouling, since its acoustic measurement does not depend on direct fluid contact with a sensitive surface in the same way. Buoys scheduled for physical servicing typically require full biofouling removal from every submerged surface, a labor-intensive process performed by the servicing vessel's crew during each maintenance visit. The research consortium is evaluating alternative anti-fouling coatings intended to extend the interval between required servicing visits, though no coating tested so far has eliminated the need for periodic manual cleaning entirely.

title: Deployment logistics
text: Deploying a new Neptune-9 buoy requires a dedicated research vessel, favorable sea conditions, and a multi-day transit to the buoy's designated mooring position. The mooring anchor is lowered first, followed by the tether and finally the buoy itself, a sequence chosen to minimize the risk of tether entanglement during deployment. Each buoy undergoes a full functional check, confirming sensor readings and satellite connectivity, before the deployment vessel departs the station. Servicing an existing buoy in place is generally faster than a full redeployment, but still requires similarly favorable weather and vessel availability, which can delay urgent maintenance by weeks during storm-prone seasons. The consortium schedules routine servicing visits around seasonal weather windows specific to the array's ocean region, rather than on a fixed calendar interval.

title: Deployment history and specifications (Neptune-9 anchor)
text: The Neptune-9 buoy array was deployed in 2018, with the full complement of 340 buoys reaching their assigned mooring positions by the end of that year's deployment season. The array covers a designated ocean region spanning roughly 900,000 square kilometers, with buoys spaced to provide meaningful spatial coverage of current and temperature variation across that area. Average buoy spacing across the array is approximately 50 kilometers, though spacing varies somewhat based on regional bathymetry and prevailing current patterns. As of the consortium's most recent public status report, 312 of the original 340 buoys remain actively reporting, with the remainder retired after reaching the end of their serviceable lifetime.

title: Data volume and network statistics
text: The array's public data feed has accumulated over 40 million individual sensor readings since deployment began, spanning temperature, salinity, current velocity, and surface meteorological measurements. Average satellite transmission success rate across the array is 97.3 percent of scheduled windows, with the remainder attributed to temporary satellite visibility gaps or buoy-side power conservation. The consortium's ground station processes incoming data continuously, publishing validated readings to the public feed within approximately fifteen minutes of successful satellite transmission. Data usage statistics indicate the array's public feed is queried most heavily by climate research groups studying regional current pattern shifts over multi-year timescales.

title: Consensus protocol experiment reference (distributed-consensus cross-reference)
text: In 2016, before the array's full production deployment, the consortium's engineering team ran a limited experimental test of the array's control-plane software using an early, pre-production subset of buoys. That experimental test evaluated whether a Byzantine fault tolerant consensus protocol could coordinate configuration updates across the buoy subset over the same intermittent satellite links used for ordinary sensor data. The experimental test used a small subset of pre-production hardware over the array's characteristic satellite link budget, well before the array's full 2018 deployment, to validate the control-plane software independent of the eventual production sensor payload. Results from that early experimental test directly informed the consortium's decision to keep the production array's control plane comparatively simple, avoiding the added complexity a full consensus protocol would introduce for a use case that did not strictly require it.

title: Sensor specifications by type
text:
Each sensor type carried by a Neptune-9 buoy has a distinct measurement range and reporting precision, summarized below. | Sensor | Measurement range | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | -2 to 35 C | 0.01 C |
| Conductivity | 0 to 70 mS/cm | 0.001 mS/cm |
| Current velocity | 0 to 3 m/s | 0.005 m/s |
| Wind speed | 0 to 60 m/s | 0.1 m/s | These specifications are supplied by the sensor manufacturers and independently verified by the consortium during each buoy's pre-deployment functional check.

title: Pre-deployment functional check
text:
Before a buoy leaves the deployment vessel, the crew completes the following functional check. 1. Confirm all sensor readings fall within expected ambient ranges for the current conditions.
2. Verify satellite uplink establishes successfully and transmits a test packet.
3. Confirm battery charge level meets the minimum threshold required for post-deployment operation.
4. Inspect all housing seals for damage sustained during transit.
5. Log the buoy's exact deployment coordinates against its assigned station position. A buoy failing any single check is held back for repair rather than deployed, even if doing so delays that station's overall deployment schedule.

title: International oceanographic sensor research
text: Long-term unattended ocean sensor network design draws on research published across several language traditions beyond the English-language oceanography literature most engineers encounter first. Watanabe and Sato's 長期海洋観測ブイにおける省電力設計 (2013) provides an early power-budget analysis that directly informed Neptune-9's solar-and-wave hybrid power system design. Morozova's Биообрастание морских измерительных буёв и методы его предотвращения (2015) formalizes the biofouling mitigation approaches the array's sensor housings implement. Konstantinou's study of τηλεμετρία δεδομένων σε απομακρυσμένα ωκεανογραφικά συστήματα (remote telemetry in oceanographic systems) provides the satellite transmission scheduling methodology referenced in the satellite telemetry section. The consortium's technical documentation cites all three works directly in its engineering-design bibliography.

title: Energy budget model
text: Neptune-9's power management system models each buoy's energy budget as a simple daily balance between expected energy input and expected consumption. Let S be expected solar input, W be expected wave-harvester input, and C be total daily consumption across sensors, onboard processing, and satellite transmission; the buoy operates sustainably whenever S + W >= C on average across a rolling multi-day window. During extended low-input periods, when S + W falls below C, stored battery capacity B depletes at a rate of C - (S + W) per day, and the power management system reduces C, primarily by cutting transmission frequency, once B falls below a configured reserve threshold. This reserve threshold is set high enough that even several consecutive low-input days will not fully deplete the battery before the power management system has already reduced consumption meaningfully. The model is deliberately conservative, since underestimating consumption or overestimating input would risk a buoy going fully dark, which requires an expensive servicing visit to resolve rather than a simple remote software adjustment.

title: History: from a single test buoy to a 340-buoy production array
text: The Neptune-9 program began in 2013 as a single prototype buoy deployed near shore, close enough to a research station for frequent physical access, specifically to validate the sensor payload and power system design before committing to any larger deployment. That initial prototype ran for eighteen months, during which the engineering team iterated through three different solar panel configurations and two different wave-energy harvester designs before settling on the combination eventually used across the full production array. The near-shore prototype phase deliberately avoided testing the satellite telemetry system under realistic open-ocean conditions, since frequent physical access made a simpler, more conventional radio link sufficient for the prototype's early goals. A second prototype phase, beginning in 2015, moved a small number of buoys further offshore specifically to validate satellite telemetry and biofouling mitigation under conditions closer to the eventual production deployment region. This second phase surfaced the biofouling problem in earnest for the first time, since the near-shore prototype's more frequent servicing schedule had never allowed fouling to accumulate to a degree that meaningfully affected sensor readings. The copper-alloy anti-fouling housing now used across the production array was developed directly in response to data collected during this second prototype phase. In 2016, ahead of the full production deployment, the engineering team also ran the limited consensus-protocol control-plane experiment described elsewhere in this document, using a small subset of pre-production hardware to validate whether a more sophisticated coordination protocol was worth the added complexity for the production array's actual requirements. That experiment's conclusion, that a simpler control plane was sufficient, shaped the production array's software architecture directly, and the consortium has not revisited a more complex coordination approach since. Full production deployment across all 340 planned buoy positions took place through 2018, following two years of engineering refinement based directly on lessons from both prototype phases. Several buoys deployed early in the 2018 season used a slightly earlier housing revision than buoys deployed later that same season, once a minor sealing improvement was validated partway through the deployment schedule. The consortium's public status reports have tracked buoy attrition since full deployment, with the array's current count of 312 actively reporting buoys reflecting gradual retirements as individual units reach the end of their serviceable lifetime. Looking back, the engineering team identifies the second, offshore prototype phase as the single most valuable period in the program's development, since nearly every design decision distinguishing the production array from the original near-shore prototype, the satellite telemetry approach, the anti-fouling housing, and the simplified control plane, traces directly back to lessons learned during that phase.

title: Summary: monitoring purpose
text: This recap restates the array's core monitoring purpose for readers skimming only the overview section. The Neptune-9 buoy array is a distributed oceanographic sensor network monitoring temperature, salinity, and current speed across a designated section of open ocean. The specific sensors responsible for each measurement are described in full in the sensor payload section rather than repeated here. Nothing about this recap changes the underlying sensor payload, which remains as specified in the overview and sensor payload sections. Researchers evaluating the array's public data feed should consult those sections directly rather than relying on this summary alone. This recap is regenerated automatically whenever the overview section's wording changes, to keep the two in sync.
